# Fermentation optimization

## Fermentation optimization: challenges and opportunities.

[Fermentation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermentation) is a key biotechnological process for the production of various bio-based products, such as fuels, fine chemicals, personal care products, agrichem products, pharmaceuticals and food ingredients. Fermentation optimization involves the intensification of microbial cell factories and strains, culture media, bioreactor design and operating parameter conditions to increase titre, productivity, yield and attributes critical to quality. However, [fermentation process development](/technologies/upstream-process) faces several challenges, such as the complexity of microbial metabolism, the variability of biological systems, biomanufacturing scale-up and technoeconomic challenges. In this article, we discuss fermentation process development, focusing on fermentation screening, optimisation, robustness testing, scale-up, fermentation characterisation, strain improvement and bioreactor optimization.

![fermentation optimization](/images/technologies/fermentation_optimization.jpg)

![fermentation optimization](/images/technologies/fermentation_optimization.jpg)

## What is fermentation optimization?

Fermentation optimization is the process of finding the optimal values of process variables, such as temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, nutrient composition, critical substrate delivery and mixing characteristics that maximize desired fermentation and commercial performance. Fermentation optimization can be performed using various methods, such as design of experiment (DOE), classical experimental design or machine-learning / AI-guided approaches. Fermentation optimization has many product-specific objectives. For instance, improving fermentation efficiency (the ratio of the product yield to substrate consumption), to reduce the production of unwanted by-products (such as ethanol, acetate, and lactate), to reduce the time of the overall process and to reduce the overall cost of the process in other ways, such as reduced costs of raw materials and unit procedures for the process. Product quality can also be improved through fermentation optimization, by finding optimum parameter ranges that facilitate production of a fully functional and highly active product. Fermentation optimization also helps to enhance the consistency and robustness of the fermentation process.

![stirred tank reactor fermentation improvement](/images/technologies/stirred-tank-reactor.jpg)

## What is Quality by Design (QbD) and how can it help with fermentation optimization and process development?

## Summary

In conclusion, fermentation optimization is a complex and multidisciplinary field that requires the integration of various aspects, such as strain engineering, media formulation, bioreactor design, process optimization, scale-up, characterization, and control. Fermentation optimization offers many challenges and opportunities for the advancement of biotechnology and the production of bio-based products. Fermentation optimization can benefit from the application of various technologies and methods, such as high-throughput screening, omics analysis, mathematical modelling, risk assessment and artificial intelligence, to accelerate the innovation and discovery of new and improved fermentation processes.

The team at Isomerase are highly experienced at fermentation optimization and are happy to [answer any questions, discuss and/or quote for your projec](/contact-us)t.

Review Article

Fermentation design and process optimization strategy based on machine learning
===============================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Zhen-Zhi Wang a, Du-Wen Zeng a, Yi-Fan Zhu a, Ming-Hai Zhou a, Akihiko Kondo b, Tomohisa Hasunuma b, Xin-Qing Zhao a

Show more

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Fermentation optimization is important for industrialization of biological manufacturing, and has been widely applied to diverse sectors including medicine, food, cosmetics and bioenergy, which is related to substantial economic benefits. Strain development is considered to be the core part of fermentation technology, as it directly influences the product yield and overall success of the fermentation process. However, fermentation design and process optimization also play a crucial role in fully exploring the genetic potential of engineered strains for efficient bioproduction. Due to the fact that fermentation process is influenced by complex factors, so far, machine learning has been widely used in this area with its strong capabilities of simulation and prediction. This review provides a brief introduction to the process of fermentation design and process optimization based on machine learning. In the workflow, experimental design strategy is fundamental to explore and characterize the performance of fermentation system. Then, machine learning modelling is employed to simulate the operation of fermentation system and the appropriate fermentation conditions, such as medium composition and process parameters, will be determined. Moreover, in recent years, some extension ideas of fermentation design based on machine learning have also been proposed, including automated fermentation process control, data mining for exploring strain characteristics, transfer learning, hybrid model building, and soft sensor construction. These strategies have created more application scenarios for machine learning, enhancing its adaptability in designing and optimizing the complex fermentation system for efficient bioproduction.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000044)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000056)

Keywords
--------

Machine learning

Fermentation optimization

Automated fermentation process control

Efficient bioproduction

Process Design

1. Introduction
---------------

Fermentation is the process by which specific metabolic pathways of microorganisms are utilized to produce metabolites. With the rapid development of industry, modern fermentation technology enables large-scale production of bio-based products. Using renewable substances as raw materials, fermentation technology contributes to sustainable economic growth, which is beneficial to carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals. Strain development is the key issue of fermentation engineering, but it is also crucial to design and optimize fermentation process from the perspective of systematic optimization to achieve efficient bioproduction.

In the fermentation process, temperature [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib2)], pH [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib4)], dissolved oxygen [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib5)], nutritional conditions (nitrogen source [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib6)], carbon source [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib7)], mineral salts and vitamins [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib8)]), feeding strategy [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib9)] and other factors will strongly impact the titer and yield of final products ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#tbl1)), which should be optimized in order to fully exploit the production capacity of engineered strains. Due to the complex and dynamic interaction between microorganisms and fermentation environment, applying mechanism models to reflect specific effects of various fermentation conditions and simulate operation of fermentation system poses a great challenge [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib10)]. For example, fermentation kinetics models are mathematical models describing cell growth, substrate utilization and product generation [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib11)]. However, these models often struggle to fully represent the complex internal metabolic processes within organisms and fail to predict the impact of factors beyond substrate concentration. Therefore, empirical models are often utilized to represent the input-output relationships between various factors and fermentation results. Empirical models are black-box models that do not consider the internal mechanisms, and methods of machine learning (ML), a subset of artificial intelligence (AI), are commonly used to construct such models [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib12)].

Table 1. Key factors in fermentation process.

| Factor | Mechanism | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Temperature | Affecting cell growth | [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib2)] |
| Changing enzyme activity |
| Affecting physical properties of fermentation broth |
| pH | Causing physiological stress | [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib4)] |
| Altering metabolic characteristics |
| Triggering chemical reactions |
| Stirring speed and ventilation | Changing dissolved oxygen levels | [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib5)] |
| Improving the overall homogeneity to promote the cell's absorption of dissolved oxygen and nutrients |
| Nitrogen source | Participating in protein synthesis | [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib6)] |
| Changing osmotic pressure and pH |
| Carbon source | Providing matter and energy for metabolism | [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib7)] |
| Changing osmotic pressure and pH |
| Mineral salts and vitamins | Supporting life activities as important cofactors | [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib8)] |
| Feeding rate | Ensuring appropriate substrate supply | [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib9)] |
| Avoiding metabolic adjustments |

ML refers to using empirical data and specific algorithms on computer to generate models with predictive functions [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib13)]. This process is data-driven, which derives statistical relationships among variables from training data. Random Forests (RF) [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib14)], Support Vector Machine (SVM) [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib15)] and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib16)] are typical ML algorithms for simulating and optimizing the fermentation process. By capturing the input-output relationship of fermentation system, these models are able to uncover the complex patterns of interaction and identify optimal fermentation conditions. In addition, ML methods hold great potential for modifying metabolic pathways in strains, which can identify potential overexpression targets for strain engineering [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib17)], screen for the best combination of biological elements [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib18)], guide the fine tuning of enzyme levels [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib19)], facilitate de novo promoter design [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib20)], and predict the impact of 5’ untranslated region on translation efficiency [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib21)].

Today, advancements in synthetic biology have accelerated the speed of strain development and iteration, imposing higher demands on the efficiency of fermentation optimization for newly engineered strains. In the era of AI, high-performance ML algorithms and high-throughput fermentation test equipment have been developed, providing impetus for rapid development in the field of fermentation optimization [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib22)]. Some existing reviews have summarized the progress of ML-based fermentation optimization [[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib25), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib27)]. However, a comprehensive introduction to the overall processes and methodologies involved is still lacking, and there is no centralized categorization of the extended applications of ML methods in this field. To meet these challenges, here we summarize the algorithms and the concrete workflow of ML-based fermentation design and optimization ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#fig1)), comprising experimental design strategy for fermentation test, ML modeling methods, model evaluation, hyperparameter tuning and prediction as well as extension ideas of ML-based fermentation design.

![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (518KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (518KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.1. The workflow of ML-based fermentation design and process optimization. Strain development is the first step of fermentation engineering. Then, fermentation test is applied to explore and characterize the performance of fermentation system. By ML modelling and prediction, the appropriate fermentation parameters will be determined. The results of fermentation optimization can be used as reference and basis for strain development. Before industrialization, scaling-up is required to expand the fermentation process that is originally conducted on a laboratory scale to an industrial level.

2. Experimental design for fermentation test
--------------------------------------------

Fermentation test serves as a cornerstone for investigating and characterizing the performance of fermentation system. So far, One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments such as removal experiments and supplementation experiments have been widely used [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib27)], despite being time-consuming and yielding unstable results. To enhance the effectiveness of fermentation test, a comprehensive process development strategy has been developed, like DOE (Design of Experiment) strategy based on statistical principles [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib28)], as depicted in [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#fig2), including Factorial Design [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib29)], Plackett-Burman Design [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib30)], Orthogonal Design [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib31)], Uniform Design [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib32)], Central Composite Design [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib33)] and Box-Behnken Design [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib34)]. The task of DOE can be facilitated by relevant software, such as Design Expert - a software tailored for experimental design developed by Stat-Ease, which can generate various experimental design schemes and conduct basic statistical analysis [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib35)].

![Image 10: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (1MB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (1MB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2693125725000032-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.2. Schematic diagram of DOE (Design of experiment) strategy. DOE is a comprehensive process development strategy, with capacity of evaluating the interactions among various factors. Capital letters represent different factors. Greek numbers in (a)–(b) and pentagram dots in (c)–(f) represent test points. Test point is a single experiment combining different levels of factors.

Compared to DOE strategy, One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments are appropriate in preliminary screening and determining the optimal level of relatively independent factors [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib36)]. However, the interaction between different factors cannot be ignored. The primary advantage of DOE lies in its capacity to evaluate the interactions among various factors while reducing the number of experiments required. For instance (see [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#tbl2)), Plackett-Burman Design is suitable for preliminary screening of various factors and identifying dominant ones, which has been widely used in exploring the effects of mineral salts [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib37),[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib38)]. Orthogonal Design and Uniform Design are used to assess the correlations among various factors with different levels [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib39),[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib40)]. Central Composite Design [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib38),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib41)] and Box-Behnken Design [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib42)] are specially used for establishing mathematical relationships between variables and outputs.

Table 2. DOE strategy and examples.

| DOE strategy | Example | Product | Productivity | Reference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Factorial Design | Identifying mineral salts with significant effect | Ethanol | 0.42​g/g sugar in 12​h from industrial wastewater | [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib8)] |
| Plackett-Burman Design | Studying the effects of supplementation of different mineral salts | Ethanol | 50.4​g/L after nutritional supplementation, 7.4-fold higher than non-supplemented | [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib37)] |
| Orthogonal Design | Optimization of temperature, fermentation time, initial pH and liquid loading volume (L 9[3 4]) | Amylase | 20.59 U/ml under optimal conditions, 7-fold higher than initial condition | [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib39)] |
| Uniform Design | Optimization of glycerol, yeast powder, peptone, KH 2 PO 4, MgSO 4, and ZnSO 4 (U 10(10 6)) | Ergosterol peroxide | 256​μg/L, 5-fold higher than initial condition | [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib40)] |
| Central Composite Design | Identifying the effects of glucose, sodium glutamate and sea salt | Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) | From 6.18​g/L to 8.33​g/L after optimization | [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib38)] |
| Box-Behnken Design | Optimization of initial pH, temperature, moisture content, and incubation period using alfa as sole carbon source | Xylanase and CMCase | 826.63 U/gds for xylanase activity, increased by 25​%. 61.17 U/gds for CMCase activity, increased by 26​% | [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib42)] |

In general, when it is not clear which factors have relatively large impacts on the fermentation results and are most worthy of optimization, one can start with an Plackett-Burman Design [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib38)], or a Minimum Run Resolution IV Design [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib41)]. However, these methods cannot quantitatively assess the optimal level of each factor compared to other DOE strategies. Uniform Design requires relatively less workload, but sacrifices a certain degree of accuracy because it does not consider the equal occurrence of different level combinations. In case of three factors each of which are to be tested with three levels, Factorial Design, Central Composite Design, Orthogonal Design and Box-Behnken Design require 27, 15, 12 and 9 experimental tests, respectively (see [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#fig2)). With a larger number of test points, the workload will increase, and the evaluation of the impact of various factors and their interactions will be more accuracy. Therefore, DOE strategy should be reasonably selected according to the requirements of accuracy and experimental conditions. The selection of factor levels also significantly influences the validity of the results; thus, it is essential to have a good understanding of the strain's characteristics and to consult relevant publications for guidance. What's more, it is important to note that when testing the influence of certain factors, it is necessary to keep other factors at an appropriate level based on experiences.

Based on DOE, traditional statistical analysis methods such as ANOVA can be employed to preliminarily assess the impact of various factors and directly select the best level, but the construction of ML models can yield more precise predictive results. There is no strict correspondence between specific ML algorithm and particular experimental design strategy. Theoretically, different experimental design strategy may be suitable for different ML algorithms. However, multiple ML algorithms can be trained on the same set of data, and the best performing model can be selected afterwards [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib43)]. Data obtained from Factorial Design, Orthogonal Design, Uniform Design, and Response Surface Methodology can all be utilized for ML model training. Furthermore, we can conduct a new batch of experiments based on previous experiment, and put them together for model construction. A typical example is constructing a new Central Composite Design experiment originating from Factorial Design [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib8)].

3. Modelling methods for fermentation optimization
--------------------------------------------------

### 3.1. Overview

After obtaining fermentation data, ML algorithms can be employed for model building to predict the fermentation results under various conditions. This necessitates a certain level of proficiency in mathematical statistics and computer technology. In the age of AI, powerful tools have emerged, which drastically lower the barrier of usage and facilitate widespread application of ML across diverse research fields. Python, as one of the most popular programming languages for ML, has powerful packages such as Scikit-learn [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib44),[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib45)]. MATLAB also has a regression model toolbox containing various ML algorithms [[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib46)].

In addition to the algorithm, data quality also represents a critical factor that impacts the accuracy of the model. Thus, the fermentation data requires preprocessing before model training [[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib47)]. For example, each feature of data, such as sugar input (g/L) and temperature (°C), has different value range, variance, and unit of measurement. By standardization or normalization, the preprocessed data preserves original characteristics, eliminates inconsistency and enhance model reliability. In the workflow of ML-based fermentation design and process optimization depicted in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#fig1), variables involved in fermentation tests are limited and hypothetically influential, so the quality of data is only affected by experimental design and the accuracy of the fermentation result detection. However, if we acquire data from fermentor or even extract information from the literature, as presented in section [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#sec5) (**Extension ideas of ML-based fermentation design**), there may be some missing data or outliers in the dataset. Then, missing value handling and data cleaning should be applied to enhance data quality.

### 3.2. Response surface methodology

Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is not a ML method in the traditional sense, but it also attempts to find the relationship between input variables and output variables. RSM is a simple and effective modelling method which can be completed by statistical analysis software [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib48)]. Based on the steepness and shape of response surface, it is possible to observe the influence of each factor and the interactions. With regression equation, the optimal fermentation conditions can be predicted [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib38),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib41),[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib42),[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib49)]. However, the model parameters constructed by RSM are relatively insufficient. A second-order model only includes the first and quadratic term (x, x 2) of each factor, as well as the interaction term between two factors (x 1 x 2), which lacks the ability to characterize the complex nonlinear interactions in fermentation system [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib48)].

### 3.3. Typical ML algorithms

Decision Tree is a ML method based on tree structure [[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2693125725000032#bib50)]. This model can o

enriched_points.expanded_content: Achieving optimal microbial cultures involves manipulating environmental conditions and utilizing advanced methods like Design of Experiment (DOE) and machine learning for enhanced yield and product quality.
Bioreactor design challenges include ensuring proper mixing, temperature control, and oxygen transfer, which are critical for maximizing microbial growth and metabolite production during scale-up.

Integrating data analytics enhances fermentation processes by predicting outcomes, optimizing conditions, and improving real-time decision-making using machine learning and statistical modeling techniques.
Contamination can severely impact fermentation quality and yield; implementing stringent sterilization protocols and monitoring techniques is essential to mitigate these risks effectively.
Variability in fermentation conditions can lead to inconsistent product quality; rigorous process control and optimization strategies are required to ensure uniformity and compliance with standards.
